I've been watching a lot of Nuggets games lately and I think people are sleeping on Michael Porter Jr's defensive improvement. He's gone from being a liability on that end to a legitimate plus defender, and the numbers back it up. According to Basketball-Reference, Porter's defensive box plus/minus has jumped from -2.3 in 2020-2021 to 0.5 this season. He's also seen significant increases in his steal and block percentages, and his overall defensive rating has dropped from 114.1 to 108.5.

What's really interesting is how Porter's defensive growth is tied to his improved footwork and closeout technique. If you watch the tape, you'll see that he's doing a much better job of staying in front of his man and contesting shots without fouling. The Nuggets are also using him more frequently in switching situations, and he's shown the ability to keep up with quicker guards. For example, in their recent win over the Grizzlies, Porter had a crucial sequence where he switched onto Ja Morant and forced him into a tough miss. It's plays like those that are making me think Porter could be a key contributor to a deep Nuggets playoff run.

The advanced metrics also suggest that Porter's defense is having a positive impact on the team's overall performance. When he's on the court, the Nuggets are allowing just 105.2 points per 100 possessions, compared to 112.1 when he's off. That's a significant difference, and it's not just due to the fact that he's playing alongside other good defenders - Porter's individual defensive numbers are holding up even when you isolate him from the rest of the team. I think it's time to start giving him more credit for his work on the defensive end, and I'm excited to see how he continues to develop as the season wears on.
label: analysis

I've been watching a lot of Nikola Jokic's game tape and I think people are sleeping on his ability to score in the post. He's got a true shooting percentage of 64.4% on post-ups this season, which is 5th in the league among players with at least 100 post-up attempts. What's even more impressive is that he's able to score efficiently against both single and double teams - his PPP on post-ups against single coverage is 1.04, and it actually increases to 1.09 when he's doubled. 

If you look at the Nuggets' offense as a whole, they're 4th in the league in points per possession on post-ups, and Jokic is the main reason why. He's got a variety of moves that he can go to, including a fadeaway jumper, a hook shot, and even a little jump hook that he's developed this season. And what's really unique about Jokic is that he's able to use his vision and passing ability to exploit double teams - he's got a 14.1% assist percentage on post-ups, which is absurdly high for a big man. 

I think the key to defending Jokic in the post is to try to cut off his right hand - he's got a much higher true shooting percentage on post-ups when he's going to his right (71.1%) compared to when he's going to his left (55.6%). The Lakers did a good job of this in their playoff series against the Nuggets last year, using a combination of Anthony Davis and Marc Gasol to defend Jokic and limit his scoring opportunities in the post. If teams can find a way to neutralize Jokic's post game, they might be able to slow down the Nuggets' offense and give themselves a chance to win.

I've been watching the Nuggets' recent games and I think their pick-and-roll defense is a major concern. Specifically, they're struggling to contain guards who can shoot off the dribble, like Damian Lillard and Stephen Curry. According to Second Spectrum, the Nuggets are allowing 1.23 points per possession on pick-and-rolls where the ball handler shoots, which ranks 25th in the league. This is largely due to their strategy of switching on screens, which leaves their big men to guard quick guards on the perimeter. Nikola Jokic, in particular, is having trouble staying in front of these guards, and it's resulting in open threes or easy layups.

In their last game against the Warriors, Curry exploited this defense repeatedly, going 5-7 from three off pick-and-rolls. The Nuggets tried to mix it up by having their guards fight over the screens, but Curry's ability to reject the screen and shoot from 28 feet made it almost impossible to defend. I think the Nuggets need to consider a different approach, like having their big men hedge harder on the screens or using more drop coverage to protect the paint. This would require a bit of an adjustment from Jokic, who would need to be more aggressive in his closeouts and trust his guards to recover to the shooter. 

It's worth noting that the Nuggets' pick-and-roll defense was a strength last season, ranking 10th in the league in points per possession allowed. However, they've lost some key personnel on the defensive end, including Torrey Craig and Jerami Grant, and it's clear that they're still trying to find their footing. With some tweaks to their strategy and a bit more cohesion, I think the Nuggets can get back to being a top-tier defensive team. They just need to figure out how to contain these high-level guards and make the necessary adjustments to their pick-and-roll defense.
label: analysis
